text.title: Process Mining Fundamentals: Algorithms, Event Logs, and Mining Types
text.summary: Discovery algorithm comparison, minimum event-log requirements, and mining question taxonomy (discovery vs conformance vs enhancement).

rule.statement: Name the mining question type (discovery, conformance, or enhancement) before selecting an algorithm; each produces a different artefact.
rule.rationale: Agents that conflate discovery and conformance produce graphs nobody asked for; naming the question type prevents wasted analysis cycles.

list.item: Discovery: reconstruct the actual process model from event data — produces a Petri net or DFG.
Conformance: compare the discovered model against a normative BPMN/Petri net — produces a fitness/precision score and deviation log.
Enhancement: enrich an existing model with performance data (cycle time, waiting time, resource load) — produces an annotated model.

section.title: Event Log Minimum Requirements

rule.statement: Audit the event log for three minimum columns (case_id, activity, timestamp) and additional quality checks before selecting any tool or algorithm.
rule.rationale: 80% of process mining project effort is log preparation; running an algorithm on an unprepared log produces an unsound or unreadable result regardless of tool quality.

list.item: Required columns: case_id (unique per process instance), activity (discrete label), timestamp (datetime, not date-only).
Minimum scale: >= 200 cases; < 3 activities or > 200 activities — taxonomy problem, not a mining problem.
Bad timestamp rate: reject if more than 2% of rows have unparseable timestamps.
Case cardinality: if one case_id spans multiple business processes, split first.
Activity vocabulary: if free-text comments, define a grouping taxonomy before mining.

antipattern.description: Proceeding to algorithm selection with only case_id/activity/timestamp columns validated, skipping timestamp format, case-ID consistency, and vocabulary size checks.
antipattern.reason: Even a structurally valid log fails when timestamps are date-only (no ordering within a day), case IDs cross process boundaries, or activity labels are free-text — each produces a spaghetti DFG or unsound Petri net.

section.reference: Python script auditing a CSV log for mining readiness and recommending an algorithm.

rule.statement: Select a single named algorithm variant with explicit rationale; reject "use whichever the tool defaults to."
rule.rationale: Alpha Miner cannot handle loops or noise; Heuristic Miner handles noise but loses formal guarantees; Inductive Miner guarantees soundness but trades fitness for precision. The wrong choice on a real-world log wastes a month of debugging.

example.note: Activity count < 15 and variant count < 30: Alpha Miner. Variant count > 100 or significant noise: Inductive Miner (infrequent variant). Moderate noise, loop presence: Heuristic Miner.
Running Alpha Miner on a real-world ITSM log with 5,000 cases and 600+ variants — Alpha cannot handle loops or noise, producing an unsound Petri net that is unreadable and invalid.

rule.statement: Disclose the algorithm name and parameters on every mining artefact; downstream readers cannot judge the graph's reliability without them.
rule.rationale: Vendors rebrand standard algorithms; the BA must press for the specific algorithm and its defaults, then record them on the deliverable.

rule.statement: Filter to top-10 variants by frequency for first review; surface the long tail only after the main flow is understood.
rule.rationale: A real ITSM log with 5,000 cases surfaces 600+ variants; the unfiltered discovery graph is unreadable, creating the "spaghetti DFG presented as success" failure mode.

rule.statement: Add a node/edge count gate (e.g., abort if more than 40 nodes without explicit override) to prevent unreadable outputs being delivered as complete work.
rule.rationale: Agents do not perceive graph unreadability; they deliver a 600-node SVG and call the task done without a structural quality gate.
